message.content: Adversaries may use binary padding to obfuscate files or information by adding junk data to malware, altering its on-disk representation without affecting functionality. This technique increases the file size, potentially exceeding the limits of some security tools and public file scanning services, thereby evading detection. Binary padding changes the file's checksum, helping to avoid hash-based blocklists and static anti-virus signatures. Commonly, adversaries generate junk data and append it to the end or within sections of the malware. Notable groups like APT29, APT32, and Gamaredon Group have employed this method to evade detection and hinder analysis. Mitigation strategies include configuring security tools to handle larger files, employing behavioral analysis over static signatures, and using advanced heuristics to detect anomalies in file structures. Regular updates to anti-virus definitions and employing multi-layered security approaches can also help counteract this technique.
